section.title: He Put a New Song in My Mouth
section.sectionRationale: David moves from testimony of a past rescue, being lifted from the pit and given a new song, to a declaration that obedience, not mere sacrifice, is what God desires, and finally to an urgent plea for present help. The Letter to the Hebrews quotes verses 6-8 directly as the words of Christ Himself entering the world.
section.thesis: David testifies that the LORD lifted him from the pit and put a new song of praise in his mouth, and declares that God desires a heart that delights to do His will rather than mere sacrifice, words the New Testament places directly in the mouth of Jesus as He comes to do the Father's will once for all.
section.frameNote: Four movements: testimony of rescue and a new song, verses 1-3; the blessing of trusting the LORD and the wonders He has done, verses 4-5; the declaration that obedience, not sacrifice, is what God desires, verses 6-10; and the closing plea for present help amid overwhelming trouble, verses 11-17.

units.label: He put a new song in my mouth

verses.text: I waited patiently for the LORD; He inclined to me and heard my cry.
He lifted me up from the pit of despair, out of the miry clay; He set my feet upon a rock, and made my footsteps firm.
He put a new song in my mouth, a hymn of praise to our God. Many will see and fear and put their trust in the LORD.

units.label: Blessed is the man who trusts the Lord

verses.text: Blessed is the man who has made the LORD his trust, who has not turned to the proud, nor to those who lapse into falsehood.
Many, O LORD my God, are the wonders You have done, and the plans You have for us, none can compare to You, if I proclaim and declare them, they are more than I can count.

units.label: I delight to do Your will

verses.text: Sacrifice and offering You did not desire, but my ears You have {{karah|opened}}. Burnt offerings and sin offerings You did not require.
Then I said, "Here I am, I have come, it is written about me in the scroll:
I delight to do Your will, O my God; Your law is within my heart."

I proclaim righteousness in the great assembly; behold, I do not seal my lips, 
as You, O LORD, do know.
I have not covered up Your righteousness in my heart; I have declared Your faithfulness and salvation; I have not concealed Your loving devotion and faithfulness from the great assembly.

units.label: Be pleased, O Lord, to deliver me

verses.text: O LORD, do not withhold Your mercy from me; Your loving devotion and faithfulness will always guard me.
For evils without number surround me; my sins have overtaken me, so that I cannot see. They are more than the hairs of my head, and my heart has failed within me.
Be pleased, O LORD, to deliver me; hurry, O LORD, to help me.
May those who seek my life be ashamed and confounded; may those who wish me harm be repelled and humiliated.
May those who say to me, "Aha, aha!" be appalled at their own shame.
May all who seek You rejoice and be glad in You; may those who love Your salvation always say, "The LORD be magnified!"
But I am poor and needy; may the Lord think of me. You are my helper and deliverer; O my God, do not delay.

karah.gloss: Literally 'ears You have dug,' picturing God opening or piercing the ear to hear and obey, an image the Greek Septuagint renders as 'a body You have prepared for me,' the very form quoted in Hebrews 10:5 of Christ's incarnation.

basic.context: David testifies to a past rescue, lifted from a miry pit, given a new song, and moves to the heart of true worship: God does not ultimately desire sacrifice and offering but a heart that delights to do His will, 'Here I am, I have come.' The psalm then turns to present, urgent need, surrounded by evils and sins, pleading for God's quick help even while trusting His steadfast love.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm holds together testimony of past deliverance, a theology of true worship as obedient delight rather than ritual performance, and honest present lament, teaching that remembering God's past faithfulness fuels present hope rather than replacing the need for it.
basic.originalLanguages: Verse 6's striking phrase, 'my ears You have opened,' literally 'dug,' pictures God piercing the ear to hear and obey rather than merely perform ritual. The Greek Old Testament renders this 'a body You have prepared for me,' the very wording Hebrews 10:5 places on Christ's lips as He enters the world.
basic.typology: David's declaration, 'Here I am, I have come... I delight to do Your will,' functions typologically as the pattern, and in Hebrews 10:5-7 explicitly as the very words, of Christ's obedient incarnation, coming to do what sacrifices and offerings alone could never accomplish.
basic.christ: Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes this psalm's verses 6-8 directly as the words Christ speaks entering the world: 'Sacrifice and offering You did not desire, but a body You have prepared for Me... Then I said, Here I am, I have come, it is written of Me in the scroll, I have come to do Your will, O God.' What David could only foreshadow in his own obedient life, Christ fulfills completely, His body offered once for all replacing every sacrifice that could never truly take away sin.
basic.god: God is shown as one who values a heart that delights in obedience over the mere performance of ritual sacrifice, and as the faithful helper whose steadfast love and faithfulness will always guard those who trust Him, even amid overwhelming present trouble.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 10:5-10 quotes this psalm directly, showing Christ's incarnation and obedience fulfilling and superseding the entire sacrificial system. 1 Samuel 15:22 similarly declares that obedience is better than sacrifice. Revelation 5:9 pictures the new song of redemption sung to the Lamb who was slain.
basic.oneStory: David's testimony of rescue, new song, and obedient delight in God's will anticipates Christ, who takes this psalm's very words on His lips in Hebrews 10, coming in a body prepared for Him to do the Father's will fully, offering Himself once for all so that God's people might sing a truly new song of salvation.

context.literary: A psalm in two clear movements, thanksgiving and testimony in verses 1-10 giving way to urgent lament and petition in verses 11-17, verses 13-17 closely paralleling Psalm 70 nearly word for word, suggesting liturgical reuse of this psalm's closing plea.
context.historical: Attributed to David, recalling a specific deliverance, 'the pit of despair, the miry clay,' now historically unidentified, used to ground present confidence even as new troubles, 'evils without number,' press in.
context.canonical: Verses 6-8 are quoted directly in Hebrews 10:5-7 as the words of Christ, making this one of the psalms most explicitly placed on the lips of the incarnate Son in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify to God's past faithfulness in rescue as grounds for present trust, to declare that obedient delight in God's will, not ritual sacrifice, is the heart of true worship, and to plead honestly for present deliverance from overwhelming trouble.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's testimony and present distress; it prescribes obedient delight in God's will as true worship over mere ritual performance, and models honest, urgent petition even while resting in God's proven faithfulness.

debates.question: Does verse 6, 'sacrifice and offering You did not desire,' teach that God rejects the entire sacrificial system He commanded in the Torah?

views.label: The verse is a strong rhetorical contrast, common in Hebrew poetry and the prophets, meaning God desires obedience more than sacrifice without formally sacrifice itself, since the Torah's sacrificial system was itself God's own institution
views.case: Similar language appears in 1 Samuel 15:22 and Hosea 6:6, comparative statements elevating obedience and mercy above ritual without abolishing the sacrificial system God Himself commanded in Leviticus.

views.label: The verse anticipates the entire sacrificial system's insufficiency and coming obsolescence, since Hebrews 10:5-10 applies it directly to Christ's single sacrifice replacing the whole system, which 'can never take away sins,' Hebrews 10:11
views.case: Hebrews 10 explicitly draws out this fuller meaning, arguing from this very psalm that the repeated sacrifices were always a shadow pointing toward Christ's one, final, sufficient offering.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse elevates obedient delight in God's will above ritual performance; read canonically through Hebrews 10, the psalm's language finds its fullest meaning in Christ's incarnation and sacrifice, which fulfills and ends the entire system of animal sacrifice.

study.originalLanguages: Verse 6's Hebrew, literally 'ears You have dug for me,' pictures God opening the ear to hear and obey. The Greek Septuagint renders this 'a body You have prepared for me,' and it is this Greek wording that Hebrews 10:5 places directly on Christ's lips as He enters the world to do the Father's will.

typology.type: David's obedient declaration, 'Here I am, I have come... I delight to do Your will,' verses 7-8
typology.fulfillment: Christ's incarnation and obedience, quoted directly in Hebrews 10:5-7, coming in a body prepared for Him to do the Father's will fully.

typology.type: The new song put in the psalmist's mouth after rescue, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: The new song of redemption sung to the Lamb who was slain, Revelation 5:9.

study.christ: Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes this very psalm as the words Christ speaks upon entering the world: 'Sacrifice and offering You did not desire, but a body You have prepared for Me... Then I said, Here I am, I have come, it is written of Me in the scroll, I have come to do Your will, O God.' What David could only anticipate in his own obedient heart, Christ fulfills perfectly and finally, His one body offered accomplishing what the entire sacrificial system, repeated endlessly yet never sufficient, could never do, Hebrews 10:10-14.
study.god: God is revealed as one who desires a heart delighting in obedience over the mere performance of ritual, and as the faithful helper whose steadfast love and faithfulness guard those who trust Him even while evils without number press in from every side.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Psalm 40:6-8 directly as Christ's words entering the world, showing His incarnation and obedience fulfill and end the sacrificial system.
Declares that to obey is better than sacrifice, echoing this psalm's elevation of obedient delight over ritual performance.
Pictures the new song of redemption sung to the Lamb who was slain, fulfilling this psalm's new song put in the rescued psalmist's mouth.

study.oneStory: David's testimony of rescue, new song, and obedient delight in doing God's will anticipates Christ, who speaks this very psalm's words upon entering the world, His body prepared to do the Father's will fully and offer the one sacrifice that ends every other, so that His people may sing the truly new song of redemption.

sermon.title: What to Do When Your Spirits Sink
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Psalm 40:1-3, showing how David's testimony of being lifted from the pit and given a new song models how believers today can wait patiently on the Lord when their own spirits sink, grounding present hope in God's proven, patient faithfulness.

quiz.q: What did the LORD do for David according to verses 1-3?

options.text: He heard David's cry, lifted him from the pit of despair, set his feet on a rock, and put a new song of praise in his mouth.
options.note: This vivid testimony of rescue and renewed praise opens the psalm and grounds everything that follows.

options.text: He ignored David's cry entirely.
options.note: The text explicitly says the LORD inclined to David and heard his cry.

options.text: He punished David further for his sin.
options.note: The passage describes rescue and a new song, not further punishment; sin is addressed later, in verse 12.

options.text: He promised David wealth but withheld rescue.
options.note: The text describes actual deliverance, being lifted from the pit, not merely a promise of future wealth.

quiz.q: What does verse 6 say God desires more than sacrifice and offering?

options.text: Ears opened to hear and obey, a heart that delights to do His will.
options.note: This declaration of obedient delight over ritual sacrifice is the psalm's theological center, quoted directly in Hebrews 10.

options.text: More frequent and elaborate sacrifices.
options.note: The verse explicitly says sacrifice and offering were not what God desired most.

options.text: Silence and withdrawal from worship.
options.note: The following verses describe proclaiming God's righteousness in the great assembly, not withdrawal from worship.

options.text: Wealth given as an offering instead of animals.
options.note: The text contrasts obedient hearts with ritual sacrifice generally, not one form of offering for another.

quiz.q: How does the Letter to the Hebrews use Psalm 40:6-8?

options.text: It quotes these verses directly as the words Christ speaks upon entering the world, His body prepared to do God's will.
options.note: Hebrews 10:5-7 places this psalm's words directly on Christ's lips, showing His incarnation and obedience fulfill it.

options.text: It never references this psalm anywhere.
options.note: Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes Psalm 40:6-8 explicitly and at length.

options.text: It uses the verses to argue that animal sacrifice should continue unchanged.
options.note: Hebrews 10 uses the psalm to argue the opposite, that Christ's one sacrifice ends the need for repeated animal sacrifices.

options.text: It applies the verses only to David personally, with no connection to Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 10:5 explicitly attributes the words to Christ Himself entering the world, not merely to David.

quiz.q: What does David confess about his own condition in verse 12?

options.text: Evils without number surround him and his own sins have overtaken him so that he cannot see, his heart has failed within him.
options.note: This honest confession of present distress and sin follows immediately after his testimony of past obedience and rescue.

options.text: He claims to be entirely free from sin or trouble.
options.note: The text explicitly confesses being overtaken by sins and surrounded by evils, not freedom from trouble.

options.text: He blames only his enemies, admitting no personal sin.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly says 'my sins have overtaken me,' a personal confession, not only blame directed at enemies.

options.text: He describes only minor, easily managed difficulties.
options.note: The language, 'more than the hairs of my head,' 'my heart has failed,' describes overwhelming, not minor, distress.

quiz.q: How does David describe himself in his closing plea, verse 17?

options.text: As poor and needy, yet confident that the Lord thinks of him and is his helper and deliverer.
options.note: This combination of humble need and confident trust closes the psalm, echoing its opening testimony of rescue.

options.text: As wealthy and self-sufficient, needing no help from God.
options.note: The text explicitly describes David as poor and needy, dependent on God's help.

options.text: As entirely without hope of any deliverance.
options.note: Despite his need, David explicitly expresses confidence that God is his helper and deliverer.

options.text: As angry at God for delaying help.
options.note: While David does ask God not to delay, the tone is urgent trust and petition, not anger at God.

questions: David testifies that God lifted him from a miry pit and gave him a new song. What 'pit' has God rescued you from, and is there a 'new song' of praise you have not yet fully sung about it?
Verse 6 says God desires obedient delight over ritual sacrifice. Where in your life might you be substituting religious performance or activity for a heart that truly delights to do God's will?

Hebrews 10 places David's words, 'Here I am, I have come to do Your will,' directly on the lips of Christ entering the world. How does knowing Jesus spoke these exact words over His own incarnation change how you read David's declaration?

David moves from testimony of past rescue directly into an honest confession of present sin and overwhelming trouble. How can remembering God's past faithfulness help you bring present struggles honestly to Him, rather than hiding them?
The psalm closes, 'I am poor and needy, may the Lord think of me.' Where do you need to trade self-sufficiency for this same honest, humble dependence on God as your helper and deliverer?

translationNotes.note: My ears You have opened translates a Hebrew phrase literally meaning 'ears You have dug,' picturing an ear opened to hear and obey; the Greek Septuagint renders this 'a body You have prepared for me,' the wording Hebrews 10:5 applies to Christ.

It is written about me in the scroll likely refers to the Torah's instructions for the king, or more broadly to Scripture's witness to God's will, which David, and ultimately Christ, comes to fulfill.
These closing verses closely parallel Psalm 70 nearly word for word, suggesting this section may have circulated and been used liturgically as its own psalm of urgent petition.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 40:1-17. Two em dashes in verse 5 were rendered as commas. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org, confirming John Piper's message on Psalm 40:1-3.
